patient_profile.treatment_requested: Prolonged exposure therapy beyond visit cap

denial_pattern_sources: ignored_physician_letter: AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Physician Survey

denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We received your provider's request on 01/09/2026 for Prolonged exposure therapy beyond visit cap related to Post-traumatic stress disorder, chronic (F43.12). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria and InterQual review standards. The documentation submitted does not demonstrate that the service is medically necessary for this member at this time. The duration of Prolonged exposure therapy beyond visit cap exceeds plan limits without new clinical review. Continuation requires updated documentation not received.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with duration excessive. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management

Standard criteria apply regardless of information previously sent to the plan.
clinical_context: The 42-year-old member has Post-traumatic stress disorder, chronic (F43.12). He has been treated under commercial coverage with Prolonged exposure therapy beyond visit cap recommended as the next appropriate step. CAPS-5 score 42; completed 12 sessions with minimal symptom change. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that duration excessive bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings (including dated scale scores in the chart) that were submitted with the original request but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if his care is delayed further.
